1: The Joseph tribes' allotment begins from the Jordan at Jericho — the logical starting point since Jericho was the entry point into Canaan and the territorial pivot between Benjamin (to the south) and Ephraim (to the north). The spring of Jericho (mê yĕrîḥô) was the primary water source for the Jordan valley town; controlling it meant controlling access to the interior valleys.
2: From Bethel/Luz to the Archite border at Ataroth. Bethel is the primary northern landmark: Jacob's dream-site (Genesis 28:19), Abraham's altar-site (Genesis 12:8), the site of Jeroboam's golden calf (1 Kgs 12:29). Its position at the boundary between Ephraim and Benjamin reflects its role as a contested religious and political center throughout the monarchic period — both tribes claimed association with it.
3: The western boundary descends through Japhletite territory to Lower Beth-horon and Gezer. Beth-horon Upper and Lower are the sites of Joshua's pursuit in ch.Joshua 10:10-11; the passes were strategically critical as the main descent from the hill country to the coastal plain. Gezer's appearance here as the western terminus of Ephraim's territory sets up the crucial failure notice at Joshua 16:10 — the western boundary was theoretically claimed but practically unoccupied.
4: 'So Manasseh and Ephraim received their inheritance.' This brief summary verse marks the end of the collective Joseph allotment description before the chapter details Ephraim's specific portion. The joint description (Joshua 16:1-3) reflects the historical reality that Ephraim and Manasseh were not always clearly distinguished in early settlement patterns; the administrative separation into two distinct tribal territories was a later consolidation.
5: Ephraim's eastern boundary runs from Ataroth-addar to Upper Beth-horon. The Ataroth-addar/Beth-horon line formed the border between Ephraim and Benjamin (Joshua 18:13). The closeness of these boundaries to Jerusalem explains why Benjamin's and Ephraim's histories are intertwined: Saul (Benjaminite) and Joshua (Ephraimite) were effectively neighbors.
6: The northern boundary reaches Michmash — famous as the site of Jonathan's single-handed assault on the Philistine garrison (1 Sam 13-14). The Michmash pass was the primary northern approach to the central hill country; its fortification by the Philistines and its liberation by Jonathan are played out in the allotment geography. The boundary continues to the Jordan on the east.
7: Janoah, Ataroth, Naarah to Jericho — Ephraim's eastern boundary descends from the hill country to the Jordan valley. Naarah ('na'aran' in 1 Chronicles 7:28) is unidentified but part of the eastern Jordan valley settlements. The boundary terminates at the Jordan, making the river Ephraim's eastern wall.
8: The Kanah Ravine (Wadi Qana) forms Ephraim's western boundary, running from Tappuah to the sea. The Kanah Ravine is an actual geographic feature in the central Shephelah, identifiable today. The river/wadi system as a boundary reflects common ANE administrative practice: natural watercourses were unambiguous, perennial boundary markers.
9: 'Some cities were set apart for Ephraim within the territory of Manasseh.' This administrative oddity — Ephraimite cities embedded in Manassite territory — reflects the settlement reality of the central highlands: the two Joseph tribes' settlements were geographically intermingled. The administrative boundary drawn in this chapter is a legal idealization of a settlement pattern that was actually more complex and interwoven.
10: The Gezer failure notice: 'the Ephraimites did not drive out the Canaanites living in Gezer.' Gezer was a major Bronze Age city-state (Tell el-Jazari) whose Canaanite rulers had maintained Egyptian vassal status; its population and fortifications made it resistant to Israelite conquest. Archaeological excavations at Gezer confirm Late Bronze Age occupation continuing without destruction into the Iron Age I period. The Canaanites were subjected to mas (forced/corvée labor) instead — a compromise that preserved Canaanite urban expertise while asserting Israelite political authority. This forced-labor arrangement became a template for Solomon's later labor levies (1 Kings 9:15-21); Gezer itself was eventually burned by Pharaoh and given as a dowry to Solomon's Egyptian wife (1 Kings 9:16-17).
